topic: weather forecast — heavy rain affecting pond levels

turns.content: friend, you can check weather for me? mekong delta area. I hear big rain coming this week

I can help you think about this! I don't have live weather data, but I can check — are you looking at a specific forecast source, or do you want general advice on preparing your farm for heavy rain? The Mekong Delta does tend to get intense downpours this time of year.
my neighbor say maybe 200mm rain next 3 day. that is a lot, you know. my pond level already high from last week rain. I worry about overflow

200mm in 3 days is serious — that's a lot of water coming fast. If your pond levels are already high, overflow is a real risk. A few things you should do right away:

1. **Open your drain gates now** to lower the water level before the rain hits. Drop it 20-30cm if you can.
2. **Check your overflow pipes and spillways** — clear any debris or blockages so water can escape if it rises too fast.
3. **Reinforce your pond banks** at any low points. Sandbags or packed earth at weak spots.

The biggest danger with overflow isn't just losing water — it's losing your stock. Shrimp will escape through any breach.
yes that is what scare me. last year big rain I lose maybe 15% shrimp from one pond. they go right through overflow into canal. very expensive lesson, you know

That's a painful loss. 15% is a lot of money swimming away. To prevent that this time:

- Put **mesh screens over your overflow outlets** if you haven't already. Fine enough to stop the shrimp but open enough to let water flow through.
- If you have any temporary netting, you can wrap it around the overflow area as a secondary barrier.
- Start draining early — don't wait until the rain starts. Better to have the level too low and let rain fill it back up than to scramble when it's pouring.
mesh screen I have but old one. hole getting big. maybe I need buy new one. what size mesh you think for shrimp pond?

For shrimp ponds, you want mesh size between **2mm to 4mm** openings — small enough to hold back even juvenile shrimp, but large enough to let water and debris pass through without clogging too fast.

If your shrimp are already grown (20-30 gram range), you could get away with 5-6mm, but smaller mesh gives you better protection across all sizes.

For material, go with **HDPE (high-density polyethylene) mesh** — it lasts longer in water than regular nylon, doesn't rust like metal, and handles the sun well. You should be able to find it at any agricultural supply shop in the delta.
okay 3mm I think good. I go buy tomorrow morning before rain start. how about aerator, friend? when heavy rain come, water temperature drop fast. that bad for shrimp too, you know

You're right to think about that. When heavy rain hits, it causes a few problems at once:

- **Temperature drops** — cold rainwater on the surface can shock the shrimp.
- **Dissolved oxygen drops** — cloudy skies mean less photosynthesis from algae, and the temperature change disrupts oxygen levels.
- **pH can swing** — rainwater is usually more acidic than your pond water.

For your aerators: **run them more during and after the rain**, especially at night. Increase aeration time by 30-50%. This helps mix the water layers so cold rain doesn't just sit on top, and it keeps oxygen levels stable.

If you can, avoid feeding heavily during the worst of the rain. Shrimp eat less when stressed, and uneaten feed will just foul the water.
yes I cut feed when rain come. shrimp not hungry when water change too fast. that is waste of feed, waste of money. I run aerator extra tonight already. maybe all night

That's smart — you clearly know your ponds well. Running the aerators all night is a good call. The electricity cost is a lot less than losing stock.

One more tip: after the heavy rain passes, check your water color and clarity. If it turns very muddy or brownish from runoff, you might want to do a partial water exchange once things settle down. That'll help stabilize pH and get the water quality back to normal.

water exchange yes, I do that after every big rain. take out maybe 20% old water, bring in fresh from canal. but friend, if canal also dirty from rain, what then? sometime canal water brown like coffee after storm, you know

Good point — if the canal water is also muddy from runoff, pumping it in defeats the purpose. In that case:

- **Wait 2-3 days** after the rain stops for the canal to settle and clear up.
- In the meantime, just rely on the aerators to keep oxygen up and the water circulating.
- If you absolutely need to exchange water and the canal is still dirty, pump into a **settling area first** if you have any spare low ground — let the sediment drop out before it reaches your ponds.

Patience is key. Better to wait for clean canal water than to rush dirty water in.
thank you friend. I go check pond bank now before dark. 4am tomorrow I check again. rain or no rain, you know, shrimp farmer never rest. hah. okay bye bye
Ha, that's the truth — shrimp don't take days off so neither do you. Stay safe out there, and I hope the rain is lighter than predicted. Good luck with the ponds!
